title: UCB — Epilepsy — Synthesis
html:
UCB Epilepsy Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-25 Studies analyzed: 8 Files parsed:

  • Briviact PET Q4 2024 Full Report (Jan 2025)
  • Briviact PET Q1 2025 Topline (May 2025, 90% sample)
  • Briviact PET KPI Topline Slides (Apr 2025)
  • Nayzilam PET W2 Topline KPI Slides (Apr 2025)
  • Fintepla SFE PET Wave 1 Full Report (Nov 2025)
  • Fintepla SFE PET Wave 1 READOUT (Dec 2025)
  • Fintepla SFE Message Recall W2 Full Results (Mar 2026)
  • Briviact Clinical Leave-Behinds (adult retention, pediatric, behavior/cognition RTE) Total content analyzed: ~470K characters across extracted PPTX files Method: Cross-study pattern matching against commercial dynamics and thesis tracker

1. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: Message Effectiveness Is Declining Across the Entire UCB Portfolio — And Their Competitors Too

This is the single most important finding. It's not a Briviact problem or a Fintepla problem — it's a market-wide phenomenon.

Briviact PET (Q4 2024 → Q1 2025):

  • Average message effectiveness dropped below neurology IA (59%) for both Briviact AND XCOPRI
  • Motivation and believability perceptions declined across ALL Briviact messages, with statistically significant drops on multiple messages
  • The "#1 prescribed branded ASM" (B2) message — previously the most recalled — fell from rank 1 to rank 4 in recall
  • XCOPRI messages also trail the IA, though some XCOPRI messages improved (its efficacy messages gained recall)

Fintepla SFE (Wave 1 → Wave 2, Q3 2025 → Q1 2026):

  • Fintepla LGS: Message effectiveness declined for FL2 (seizure reduction), FL1 (broad spectrum efficacy), FL3 (side effects) — the three biggest drops
  • Fintepla DS: FD1 (profound reduction) and FD2 (seizure-free days) both declined in effectiveness
  • Epidiolex messages ALSO declined: several efficacy messages dropped in motivation, particularly broad-spectrum efficacy (ED5) and convulsive seizure reduction (ED1, ED2)
  • Only the unique MoA message (FL11/FD11) held or improved — it's the lone message swimming against the tide

Nayzilam PET:

  • Nayzilam messages actually improved recall between Q4 2024 and Q1 2025 (N8 simple dosing: 51% → 63%; N5 access: 49% → 63%)
  • Nayzilam effectiveness is on par with neurology standards — the only UCB brand currently meeting benchmarks
  • But VALTOCO messages trail IA, suggesting the rescue therapy space may be more receptive to messaging overall

What this means together: The neurology field is experiencing messaging fatigue. HCPs who see 70+ branded neurology products promoted are less responsive to product messages generally. This is not a rep quality problem or a message design problem — it's an attention economy problem. The implication for ZoomRx: stop optimizing individual messages and start studying what types of interactions cut through (high-impact detail analysis, which the Fintepla Wave 2 study begins to address).

Pattern 2: Fintepla's MoA Message Is a Breakout — The Only Message Gaining Ground Across Both Indications

Across 12 Fintepla messages (LGS) and 11 Fintepla messages (DS), plus 20+ Epidiolex messages, the unique MoA message (FL11/FD11 — "An MOA different from other ASMs") is the only one that:

  • Achieved the highest message effectiveness in both indications in both waves
  • INCREASED in effectiveness from Wave 1 to Wave 2 (while everything else declined)
  • Sits in the "Most Impactful" quadrant (high recall + high effectiveness) in both indications

LGS ME scores: FL11 MoA = 66% in Q1 2026 (vs 63% in Q3 2025) — ABOVE rare disease IA of 62% DS ME scores: FD11 MoA = 64% in Q1 2026 (vs 72% in Q3 2025) — slight decline but still highest

Why this message works: In a market where most ASMs share overlapping efficacy claims (seizure reduction, broad-spectrum efficacy), the MoA message creates genuine differentiation. Fenfluramine's serotonergic mechanism (5-HT2C + sigma-1) is truly unlike any other ASM. HCPs find this credible, motivating, AND differentiating — the trifecta that other messages can't achieve.

ZoomRx consulting implication: UCB should consider building the entire Fintepla commercial narrative around the MoA story. Efficacy messages should be reframed as consequences of the unique MoA rather than standalone claims. This is a strategic recommendation that goes beyond message testing.

Pattern 3: The Comfort-to-Recommend Gap Is Closing for Fintepla, Creating a Leading Indicator of Growth

Two metrics are moving in opposite directions for Fintepla:

Growing (positive):

  • Likelihood to recommend to a colleague: LGS 80% (up from prior wave), DS comparable to Epidiolex
  • Likelihood to increase prescribing: DS now HIGHER than Epidiolex, LGS comparable
  • At-Home ECHO awareness: ~48% (up from ~40%)
  • AES awareness: ~33% (up from ~20%)
  • Eligible patient identification during details: improved significantly

Stuck (the gap):

  • Comfort starting a patient on Fintepla: ~40% — significantly lower than Epidiolex, not meaningfully changed between waves

What this means: HCPs are intellectually convinced (they'd recommend, they'd increase Rx) but operationally hesitant (they're not comfortable actually starting the patient on Fintepla). This is a classic adoption gap: the barrier is process, not science. The REMS, the echocardiogram scheduling, the unfamiliarity with the start process — these are the blockers.

Why this matters for growth: Fintepla's 30% YoY revenue growth shows the brand IS growing despite this gap. The growth is coming from: (a) HCPs who have crossed the comfort barrier and are prescribing more, and (b) new prescribers converted by the field force. If the comfort gap narrows even modestly (40% → 55%), the revenue growth rate could accelerate significantly.

ZoomRx study design implication: The SFE study should add a "start process" deep-dive: What specific steps are uncomfortable? Is it the REMS enrollment? The echo scheduling? The first-dose monitoring? Getting this granularity would make the study 10x more actionable for UCB.

Pattern 4: Briviact Is Losing the Promotional Activity War to XCOPRI — And Frequency Is Dropping

Across two quarters of PET data, the sales force activity picture for Briviact is concerning:

Q4 2024:

  • XCOPRI reach: 85% | Briviact reach: 63%
  • XCOPRI SOV: 33% | Briviact SOV: 26%
  • Frequency: Comparable at ~1.8-1.9

Q1 2025:

  • XCOPRI SOV: 39% (up) | Briviact SOV: 21% (down)
  • Briviact reach: ~64% (stable) but frequency dropped to 1.2 (from 1.9)
  • XCOPRI reps significantly outperform Briviact reps on: ability to address questions, rep engagement, knowledge of competitor products

Q1 2025 reps performance gap: Briviact rep quality ratings dropped below neurology benchmarks on overall sales call quality, while XCOPRI stayed on par. This is new — in Q4, both brands were at parity.

What this means: UCB appears to be de-investing in Briviact personal promotion (lower frequency, declining quality ratings) while SK Life Science is doubling down on XCOPRI (higher SOV, first national TV campaign in May 2026). The multi-product detail structure (Briviact + Nayzilam) may be diluting Briviact airtime further.

One bright spot: Briviact rep closing rates improved significantly in Q1 2025. Reps are asking HCPs to prescribe more often, and are specifically asking for 1L adoption. This suggests UCB may be shifting strategy from "reach more HCPs" to "convert the ones we reach" — a rational response if they're reducing field force investment.

Pattern 5: Nayzilam's Clinical Superiority Is Real but Commercially Irrelevant Against VALTOCO's Scale

This is the starkest disconnect in the UCB portfolio:

Nayzilam wins on every quality metric:

  • Higher message recall vs matched themes (simple dosing, seizure cessation, $20 copay)
  • Higher message effectiveness vs VALTOCO
  • Higher overall sales call quality ratings
  • Higher effectiveness scores (on par with IA) while VALTOCO trails IA

VALTOCO wins on every commercial metric:

  • $295-300M in 2025 sales (doubled from 2024)
  • 87% reach vs 72% for Nayzilam
  • Higher SOV
  • Ages 2+ vs 12+ (critical pediatric advantage)
  • 12,000+ new pediatric prescriptions post-age expansion
  • Patent protection through 2032

The implication: ZoomRx PET is documenting a situation where the better-detailed product is losing to the better-distributed product. The PET study is measuring Nayzilam "winning" on everything we test, while VALTOCO runs away on everything we don't (distribution, pediatric volume, overall market share). This is a case where the research design needs to acknowledge the structural dynamics — and the consulting conversation should shift toward what levers UCB has beyond messaging.

Pattern 6: Unaided Recall Is Shifting From Monitoring to Efficacy for Fintepla — A Positive Inflection

Between Wave 1 and Wave 2 of the Fintepla SFE:

LGS unaided recall changes:

  • Monitoring requirements: DECLINED significantly
  • Efficacy/seizure control: Remains highest, stable
  • Approved indications: DECLINED

DS unaided recall changes:

  • Efficacy/seizure control: INCREASED (now higher for Fintepla than Epidiolex)
  • Monitoring requirements: DECLINED significantly
  • Safety: DECLINED
  • Patient support: DECLINED

What this means: KAMs are successfully shifting the conversation from "here's what you have to monitor" to "here's what the drug does for patients." This is exactly the behavior UCB wanted. The monitoring narrative was dominant in Wave 1 because it was NEW (Fintepla was recently launched). Now that HCPs have absorbed the monitoring requirements, the efficacy message is landing more prominently.

Critical nuance: When HCPs DO mention cardiac monitoring in Q1 2026, they often mention it alongside the support infrastructure (At-Home ECHO, REMS navigation). This reframing — from "monitoring burden" to "monitoring support" — is a signal of commercial maturation.

2. Cross-Portfolio Strategic Observations

The UCB Epilepsy Portfolio Has Three Very Different Commercial Trajectories

Brand Trajectory Strategic Need
Briviact Mature, slowing, generic threat Narrative reinvention or managed decline
Fintepla High growth, comfort gap bottleneck Comfort gap closure + CDKL5 expansion
Nayzilam Clinically strong, commercially constrained Pediatric expansion or accept niche position

ZoomRx Research Implications

  1. Briviact PET: Consider shifting from quarterly to semi-annual if message fatigue continues. The incremental value of Q-o-Q tracking diminishes when nothing is changing. Alternatively, propose a barrier-based study (non-user deep dive) to understand why HCPs who see Briviact reps still don't switch from generic levetiracetam.

  2. Fintepla SFE: The richest study in the portfolio. Add comfort-process granularity in Wave 3. Track the MoA message as the anchor point. Begin thinking about CDKL5 pre-launch measurement. This study should expand, not contract.

  3. Nayzilam PET: The data tells a clear story — Nayzilam is clinically preferred but commercially outgunned. Propose a pediatric utilization study or an access/formulary tracking study to complement the PET.

  4. Cross-portfolio opportunity: UCB has no integrated view of how the multi-product detail works. A holistic study that measures how Briviact+Nayzilam combined details impact both brands would be novel and valuable.

3. Data Confidence Assessment

Study Confidence Rationale
Briviact PET Q4 2024 High Full report, final data, 66 Briviact + 30 XCOPRI details
Briviact PET Q1 2025 Medium-high 90% sample (58/65 target), topline only
Nayzilam PET W2 Medium-high Full KPI deck, 30 Nayzilam + 16 VALTOCO (low sample for VALTOCO)
Fintepla SFE W1 High Full report, 60 Fintepla + 60 Epidiolex details, 56 unique HCPs
Fintepla SFE W2 High Full results, 60 Fintepla + 64 Epidiolex details, 57 unique HCPs
Clinical leave-behinds Supporting Approved promotional materials, not primary research
